_comment: THE DECLARATION. This file -- not the code -- is the artifact you maintain
with SMEs. The parser obeys it and refuses anything it does not declare.
canonical     : the business level name (order in this list IS the level).
aliases       : source column headers that map into this level.
vocabulary    : the CLOSED set of valid members. null = open domain (no
normalization : which CONDITIONALLY-safe healings are licensed for this
level. Verified at load: a license that would merge two
declared members is rejected before any file is read.
Universally-safe healing (unicode NFKC, zero-width chars, whitespace) is
ALWAYS on and needs no license -- it can never merge two distinct members.
A member that is not declared here BLOCKS THE LOAD. That includes genuinely
new members: adding a department is a business decision, so it is made here,
by a person, with a name and a date on it -- not by a cell in a spreadsheet.
US-LOCALE ASSUMPTION. total_labels, aggregation_prefixes and blank_member_literal
are the strings a US-English Excel emits. They live here rather than in code so
that a localized workbook is a five-minute config edit, not a two-day debug.

overrides._comment: Levels a NAMED report is explicitly permitted to lack. Keyed by report/sheet name or file basename. Scoped deliberately: a blanket switch would also forgive real mapping mistakes.

registry._comment: Where the member register lives. Machine-appended, human-approved, never hand-edited. Only used by levels in mode 'registry'.

_modes._comment: GOVERNANCE MODE per hierarchy level. Choose by CARDINALITY.
_modes.closed: You hand-write 'vocabulary'. For small, stable, high-blast-radius levels (3-20 members). Undeclared member -> load blocked.
_modes.registry: The list builds itself in members.json; a NEW member blocks until approved (in bulk). For granular, churning levels. Set 'bootstrap': true for the FIRST load only, then turn it off.
_modes.observed: No membership check. Near-duplicate detection + cardinality drift only. For levels too granular to gate.
